items.myth: Feature engineering replaces the need for scaling because log and ratio transforms already normalize data.
items.reality: Log and ratio steps change distribution shape but engineered columns can still have vastly different variances; distance- and penalty-based models need a scaler branch unless the algorithm is scale-invariant (trees).
items.example: `log1p(revenue)` and `tenure_days` after engineering still differ in spread; Ridge without StandardScaler penalizes the wider column unfairly.

items.myth: If preprocessing already scaled raw columns, engineered features inherit fair scales automatically.
items.reality: New engineered columns (sums, ratios, interactions) are computed after initial scaling or from raw sources and need their own scale decision in the FE pipeline branch.
items.example: Scaling raw `spend` then engineering `spend_per_visit = spend / visits` reintroduces unit imbalance because the ratio uses unscaled or differently scaled parents.

items.myth: The feature-engineering topic should redefine StandardScaler and MinMaxScaler from scratch.
items.reality: Canonical scaler definitions and formulas live in scaling and normalization; the FE topic links there and owns pipeline placement and algorithm-sensitive include/skip rules.
items.example: For z = (x − μ) / σ details, read data-preprocessing--scaling-and-normalization; here you wire where that transform runs after engineered numerics.
